sections.title: What is Maternal Health?
sections.content: Maternal health is all about the health and wellness of people with female bodies during pregnancy, childbirth, and the time right after giving birth (the postpartum period). It’s super important because when someone carrying a baby is healthy, it makes things safer for both them and the baby. Pregnancy brings a lot of changes to the body, some expected and some risky, like high blood pressure, infections, or bleeding. Without proper care, these can become serious or even life-threatening. Skilled healthcare workers and post-birth care are vital to prevent complications like infections, blood clots, or postpartum depression. According to WHO, about 295,000 people die yearly from pregnancy-related issues, mostly in places with limited healthcare access—most of which are preventable. Maternal health depends on access to care, education, financial stability, and community support. Improving it requires better healthcare access, skilled professionals, education, and emotional and social support.

sections.title: Maternal Health in Zambia
sections.content: Zambia has made progress in maternal healthcare with free services and more trained attendants. Antenatal care is now common, but rural areas still face challenges—distance, transport, and under-resourced clinics cause delays. Safe Motherhood Action Groups (SMAGs) help educate communities about antenatal care, skilled birth, and postnatal support. Continued investment and awareness can further reduce maternal deaths and make childbirth safer.

subsections.title: What is Preconception Care?
subsections.content: Preconception care is the healthcare received before pregnancy to prepare body and mind for a healthy pregnancy. It includes eating well (especially folic acid), avoiding harmful substances, and managing existing health conditions like diabetes or hypertension. Preparing early helps reduce complications during pregnancy.

subsections.title: Family Planning & Contraception
subsections.content: Family planning helps people decide if, when, and how to have children. There are many contraception options to choose from, and it's important to find one that fits your needs and lifestyle.

subsections.title: First Trimester (0–12 Weeks)
subsections.content: This early stage involves major changes in the body and baby. Signs include missed periods, nausea, fatigue, and mood swings. By week 6, the baby’s heart beats; by week 8, major organs begin forming. Important care includes starting prenatal visits, taking folic acid, and getting the first ultrasound.

subsections.title: Second Trimester (13–26 Weeks)
subsections.content: Nausea often fades, and energy improves. The baby’s movements begin between weeks 18–22. Routine tests check for gestational diabetes and anemia. Continue prenatal vitamins and maintain emotional support.

subsections.title: Third Trimester (27–40 Weeks)
subsections.content: The baby grows rapidly and prepares for birth. Expect discomforts like swelling, back pain, or sleep issues. Watch for signs of labor such as contractions or water breaking. Prepare for birth with check-ups and a hospital bag.

subsections.title: Stage 1: Early & Active Labour
subsections.content: Labour begins with contractions that open the cervix. Early labour features mild contractions; active labour involves stronger, closer contractions as the cervix dilates to 7cm. Healthcare teams monitor contractions and the baby’s heartbeat.

subsections.title: Stage 2: Pushing & Birth
subsections.content: Once the cervix reaches 10cm, pushing begins. It may last minutes or hours. The baby’s head emerges first, followed by the body. After birth, skin-to-skin contact supports bonding and warmth.

subsections.title: Stage 3: Delivering the Placenta
subsections.content: The placenta is delivered about 30 minutes after birth. The provider ensures it’s fully removed to avoid complications and monitors bleeding and uterine contraction.

subsections.title: Pain Management During Labour
subsections.content: Pain relief options include natural methods (breathing, movement, warm water) and medical ones (epidural, opioids, spinal block). C-sections may be necessary for complications like breech position or stalled labour.

subsections.title: Physical Recovery
subsections.content: After vaginal birth, soreness and cramping are normal; use ice packs and rest. After C-section, care for the incision and avoid heavy lifting. Recovery may take 6–8 weeks.

subsections.title: Mental Health
subsections.content: Mood swings are common after birth (baby blues). Postpartum depression is more serious and needs medical attention. Symptoms include sadness, numbness, and trouble bonding with the baby.

subsections.title: Breastfeeding
subsections.content: Breast milk offers ideal nutrition and immunity. Challenges include sore nipples or low supply. Lactation consultants or support groups can help.

subsections.title: Birth Control
subsections.content: Pregnancy can occur soon after birth. Safe options during breastfeeding include IUDs, implants, and certain pills. Consult a healthcare provider to choose the right method.

subsections.title: Newborn Screenings
subsections.content: Newborns are tested within 24–48 hours for genetic and metabolic conditions. Early detection allows timely treatment.

subsections.title: Vaccinations
subsections.content: Vaccines protect against diseases like hepatitis B, DTP, and polio. Early immunization builds strong immunity.

subsections.title: Feeding & Nutrition
subsections.content: Babies feed every 2–3 hours. Breastfeeding or formula are both healthy options. Watch for hunger cues like hand sucking or rooting.

subsections.title: Sleep & Safety
subsections.content: To reduce SIDS risk, always place the baby on their back, use a firm mattress, and keep the crib free from pillows or toys.

sections.title: High-Risk Pregnancy & Complications
sections.content: Some pregnancies are high-risk due to conditions like gestational diabetes, preeclampsia, or preterm labour. These require closer monitoring and specialized care to ensure the safety of both parent and baby.

sections.title: Maternal Health Myths & Facts
sections.content:
Myth: You can’t exercise while pregnant. Fact: Moderate exercise is safe and recommended.
Myth: Eating for two means double portions. Fact: Only 300–500 extra calories are needed.
Myth: Heartburn means a hairy baby. Fact: It’s caused by hormones, not hair.
Myth: All pregnant people crave food. Fact: Everyone’s experience is different.
